sections.text: This page accepts real visitor suggestions via a POST to /api/comment. An Inngest function orchestrates the pipeline: Claude Haiku moderates the suggestion, Claude Sonnet generates a structured patch validated by Zod schemas, Octokit commits the patch and opens a pull request, CI runs an allowlist check on every changed file path, and GitHub auto-merges on pass. Vercel rebuilds on merge. End-to-end latency is 30–90 seconds. No human is in the loop unless a comment is held for owner review.

items: POST /api/comment — suggestion is received, IP hashed (SHA-256), rate-limited, and a row inserted to Supabase with status queued; an event is dispatched to Inngest
Kill switch check — settings table is read; if the kill switch is active, the comment is marked failed before any API call is made
Moderation — Claude Haiku returns a verdict of safe, unsafe, or off-topic; anything not safe halts the pipeline here
Generation — Claude Sonnet receives current content, the target edit_id, and the suggestion; it calls the update_sections tool and returns a typed patch
Validation — the patch is run through Zod schemas; bad shapes, invalid enum values, or oversized fields throw and mark the comment failed
Commit — Octokit creates a branch, writes the patched file to content/sections.json or theme/tokens.json, and opens a PR with message format agent(edit_id): description; auto-merge is enabled
CI — GitHub Actions inspects every file in the PR diff against an allowlist of content/, theme/, and overrides/; any path outside the allowlist fails the workflow and blocks merge
Deploy — Vercel rebuilds on merge; ISR with revalidate=60 propagates the update to visitors within 60 seconds

sections.body: A GitHub Actions workflow blocks any pull request that touches files outside a strict allowlist (content/, theme/, overrides/). The check runs on GitHub's infrastructure, independent of the agent pipeline. Even a malicious or malformed patch cannot reach application code — the CI wall is enforced at the repository level, not inside the AI models.
sections.title: The agent cannot break the site

sections.text: How the CI allowlist check actually works

When Octokit opens a PR, GitHub Actions triggers a workflow that calls the GitHub API to fetch the full list of changed file paths in the diff. Each path is matched against the allowlist: content/, theme/, and overrides/. If any single path falls outside those prefixes — whether introduced intentionally or by a malformed patch — the workflow exits with a non-zero code, the CI check fails, and GitHub's branch protection rules prevent auto-merge from proceeding. The Inngest step that enabled auto-merge has no ability to override this: the check runs on GitHub's infrastructure, not inside the agent's execution context.

items: Rate limiting — 3 submissions/hour for anonymous users, 20/hour for GitHub-authenticated users, tracked by SHA-256 hashed IP stored in Supabase
AI moderation gate — Claude Haiku screens every suggestion before any Sonnet call; unsafe or off-topic content is rejected at the cheapest step in the pipeline
Zod schema validation — the agent's output is validated against strict schemas before any commit; bad shapes, missing required fields, and out-of-range values are all caught here
Element locking — while a comment is in-flight, its target edit_id is locked; further submissions for the same element return HTTP 409 until the comment resolves
Kill switch — reads the kill_switch row in the settings table; if active, the Inngest function halts before making any Anthropic API call
Anthropic spend cap — a hard monthly limit configured in the Anthropic console; when reached, the API returns errors and the pipeline marks comments failed
Held-comment queue — low-confidence outputs or sensitive edits are held for owner review; on approval, the patch is re-generated against current file state to avoid stale-patch merge conflicts

sections.body: The agent cannot invent new section types, write arbitrary HTML, or modify styling beyond the token schema. It calls update_sections with typed structured data that pre-registered renderer components know how to render. The renderer registry is hardcoded in application code — outside the agent's write scope entirely.
sections.title: What the agent cannot do

sections.text: Structural edits are not limited to text substitution. The agent can split one section into multiple nodes, merge adjacent sections, reorder the sections array, insert a new typed section, change a section's type field (e.g. paragraph → callout), or set visible: false to hide a section — all by manipulating the JSON array that the renderer consumes.

sections.code: agent(elevator-pitch): rewrite opening paragraph to active voice

items: "Add a section about the kill switch"
"Put the pipeline steps after the callout"
"Make the safety callout a success tone"

sections.text: The thing is interesting because it's real, not because it claims to be.

sections.attribution: docs/system-reference.md — on what this workflow actually does
sections.text: A suggestion can split a paragraph into three, reorder sections, add a callout, or remove a section entirely. A pure database cannot rearrange page structure. Every change has a real GitHub PR, real CI, and a real deploy — because real work is happening at each step.
Every change has a real pull request →
